burns.title: Burns First Aid
burns.severity_note: For large or severe burns, call emergency services immediately.
steps: Cool the burn immediately under cool (not cold/ice) running water for 10-20 minutes.
Remove any jewelry, watches, or tight clothing near the burn area (before swelling starts).
Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedies on the burn.
Cover loosely with a clean, non-fluffy material like cling wrap or a clean plastic bag.
Take Paracetamol for pain relief if needed.
Seek medical attention for burns larger than the palm of the hand, or on face, hands, feet, genitals or joints.

cuts.title: Cuts & Wounds First Aid
cuts.severity_note: Deep wounds or cuts that don't stop bleeding need emergency care.
steps: Apply firm pressure on the wound with a clean cloth or bandage for 10-15 minutes without lifting.
Elevate the injured part above heart level to reduce bleeding.
Once bleeding stops, clean the wound gently with clean water.
Apply an antiseptic like Dettol or Betadine to prevent infection.
Cover with a sterile bandage.
Do NOT remove objects embedded in the wound.
Get a tetanus shot if the wound is deep or if last shot was more than 5 years ago.

fainting.title: Fainting / Loss of Consciousness
fainting.severity_note: If the person does not regain consciousness within 1 minute, call emergency services.
steps: Lay the person on their back on a flat surface.
Elevate legs 8-12 inches above heart level (unless head/neck injury is suspected).
Loosen tight clothing around neck and chest.
Check if the person is breathing normally.
Do NOT give water or food until the person is fully conscious.
If breathing stops, begin CPR if trained.
When they recover, have them sit up slowly to avoid re-fainting.

choking.title: Choking First Aid
choking.severity_note: If the person cannot breathe, cough or speak, act immediately.
steps: Encourage the person to cough forcefully if they can cough.
If coughing doesn't help, stand behind the person and give 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand.
If back blows don't work, perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver): Stand behind, wrap arms around waist, make a fist and thrust inward-upward just above navel.
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until object dislodges.
If person becomes unconscious, call emergency and begin CPR.
For infants under 1 year: Use 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts – NO abdominal thrusts.

fracture.title: Fracture / Broken Bone First Aid
fracture.severity_note: Always seek medical attention for suspected fractures.
steps: Stop any bleeding by applying pressure with a clean cloth. Do not push the bone back in.
Immobilize the injured area – do NOT try to straighten the bone.
Apply a splint using firm material (rolled newspaper, wood) to keep the area still.
Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling. Do NOT apply ice directly to skin.
Elevate the limb if possible.
Take the person to hospital immediately. For spine/neck injuries, do NOT move the person unless absolutely necessary.

heart attack.title: Heart Attack First Aid
heart attack.severity_note: EMERGENCY: Call 112 immediately. Do not drive yourself.
steps: Call emergency services (112) immediately.
Help the person sit or lie in a comfortable position (usually sitting up).
Loosen tight clothing.
If person is not allergic and conscious, give 300mg Aspirin to chew (not swallow whole).
Stay with the person and keep them calm.
If person becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR if trained.
Do NOT give water, food, or any other medicines.

stroke.title: Stroke First Aid
stroke.severity_note: EMERGENCY: Use FAST test and call 112 immediately. Time is critical.
steps: Use FAST test: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency.
Call 112 immediately. Note the time symptoms started.
Keep the person calm and still. Help them lie down with head and shoulders slightly raised.
Do NOT give food or water.
If unconscious but breathing, place in recovery position (on their side).
If breathing stops, begin CPR.
Do NOT give aspirin – unlike heart attack, aspirin can worsen some strokes.

allergic reaction.title: Severe Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis) First Aid
allergic reaction.severity_note: EMERGENCY: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening. Call 112 immediately.
steps: Call emergency services (112) immediately.
If person has an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen), use it immediately on outer thigh.
Have the person lie down with legs elevated (unless breathing is difficult – sit up then).
If they stop breathing, perform CPR.
A second epinephrine dose can be given after 5-15 minutes if symptoms persist.
Do NOT give antihistamines as primary treatment – they work too slowly for anaphylaxis.

eye injury.title: Eye Injury First Aid
eye injury.severity_note: Seek immediate medical care for chemical exposure or foreign body that cannot be flushed out.
steps: Do NOT rub the eye.
For chemical splash: Immediately flush the eye with cool clean water for 15-20 minutes. Remove contact lenses before flushing.
For foreign body (dust/debris): Blink repeatedly to flush out. Try flushing with clean water.
For cuts/punctures to the eye: Cover loosely with a clean cloth. Do NOT rinse or remove objects.
Seek medical attention promptly for any significant eye injury.
Do NOT use eye drops unless instructed by a doctor.

nose bleed.title: Nosebleed First Aid
steps: Sit upright and lean slightly forward (not backward) to prevent blood from going down throat.
Pinch the soft part of the nose (just below the bony bridge) firmly.
Hold for 10-15 minutes without releasing pressure.
Breathe through your mouth.
Apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose.
Do NOT tilt head back.
After bleeding stops, do not blow nose for several hours.
See a doctor if bleeding doesn't stop after 20-30 minutes, or if it's accompanied by a head injury.

electric shock.title: Electric Shock First Aid
electric shock.severity_note: EMERGENCY: Do NOT touch the person if they are still in contact with the electricity source.
steps: Do NOT touch the person if they may still be in contact with the electrical source.
Turn off the electricity at the mains/fuse box if possible.
Call emergency services (112) immediately.
Once safe to touch, check if the person is conscious and breathing.
If unconscious and not breathing, begin CPR if trained.
Treat any burns with cool water.
Keep the person warm and still until help arrives.
Even if the person seems fine, they should be assessed at a hospital as heart rhythm problems can develop later.

heat stroke.title: Heat Stroke First Aid
heat stroke.severity_note: Heat stroke is a medical emergency. Call 112 immediately.
steps: Move the person to a cool, shaded area immediately.
Call emergency services (112).
Remove excess clothing.
Cool the person rapidly: apply ice packs to armpits, groin, neck; fan while misting with cool water.
If conscious, give cool water or sports drink to sip – NOT cold water in large amounts.
Do NOT give fever-reducing medicines (paracetamol, ibuprofen) – they do not help in heat stroke.
Monitor breathing and consciousness until help arrives.

drowning.title: Drowning First Aid
drowning.severity_note: Call 112 immediately after rescuing the person.
steps: Ensure YOUR safety first before attempting rescue.
Call 112 immediately.
Remove person from water carefully.
If unconscious and not breathing, begin CPR immediately (30 chest compressions, 2 rescue breaths).
Do NOT do the old 'drain water' technique – begin CPR directly.
If the person is breathing, place in recovery position on their side.
Keep them warm (hypothermia risk even in warm water).
All near-drowning victims should be evaluated at a hospital even if they seem fine.

poisoning.title: Poisoning First Aid
poisoning.severity_note: Call Poison Control: 1800-11-6117 (India). Call 112 for severe cases.
steps: Call Poison Control helpline: 1800-11-6117 (India, toll-free) or 112 immediately.
If the person is conscious, try to identify what was ingested and how much.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by poison control.
For skin contact with poison: Remove contaminated clothing and rinse skin with water for 15-20 minutes.
For eye contact: Rinse eye with water for 15-20 minutes.
For inhaled poison: Move person to fresh air immediately.
Bring the poison container or a list of what was ingested to the hospital.
